output:
# Per-Feature Quantum-Kernel Bandwidth: From a Single Global Scaling to a Structured, Partially-Pooled, Covariate-Conditional Aperture (ISOBAND / APERTURE)

A research-idea proposal in the 5-question (5q) format. The proposal identifies a representational impossibility in quantum kernel methods - the use of one global bandwidth scalar to scale every input feature in the data-encoding circuit - and proposes APERTURE, a per-feature bandwidth that is a structured, monotone, partially-pooled map of an observable per-feature relevance covariate, with a provable exact collapse to the global-bandwidth quantum kernel at its default setting.

Notation used throughout (ASCII): x = (x_1,...,x_d) is a classical input; y its label; c is the global bandwidth scalar; c_j the per-feature bandwidth; c_global the shared base bandwidth; v_j an observable per-feature covariate (relevance); g_theta a monotone map with parameters theta; K_c(x,x') the quantum fidelity kernel at bandwidth c; n the qubit count; E_collapse the exact-collapse gate quantity. "<=" means less-than-or-equal, ">=" greater-than-or-equal, "->" maps-to/tends-to.

---

## 1. Background and Current State of the Art

### 1.1 Quantum machine learning and the kernel viewpoint

Quantum computing has moved from foundational algorithms to a broad applied program spanning Hamiltonian simulation, spectroscopy, and combinatorial optimization, and - most relevant here - quantum machine learning (QML), in which a parameterized quantum circuit is trained to minimize a loss in analogy with classical learning. The supervised branch of QML splits into two closely related families. The first is the variational quantum classifier, in which a data-encoding circuit is followed by a trainable ansatz and a measurement, trained end-to-end like a neural network [6, 7, 17]. The second is the quantum kernel method, in which a classical input x is embedded into a quantum state through a data-encoding unitary U(x), and the model uses the fidelity kernel

    K(x, x') = | <0| U(x)^dagger U(x') |0> |^2,

feeding this Gram matrix into a classical support vector machine (SVM) or other kernel learner [1, 2, 4]. A key unifying result is that essentially all supervised QML models that linearly read out a measurement are kernel methods: the encoding U(x) fixes a feature map into a (generally exponentially large) Hilbert space, and training reduces to learning in that fixed feature space [3]. This is precisely the perspective taken by the source Introduction's reference 37 [3] and motivates studying the data-encoding step as the primary design lever.

The promise of quantum kernels is that the embedding can realize feature spaces that are classically hard to compute, yielding potential advantage when the kernel is well aligned with the target function. Rigorous separations exist for carefully constructed problems with built-in structure (for example, discrete-log-based concept classes) [10], and covariant kernels exploit group structure in the data [16]. Hardware demonstrations on superconducting and other platforms have run quantum kernels and variational classifiers on real and PCA-reduced image data [1, 19].

### 1.2 Data encoding, expressivity, and the bandwidth hyperparameter

How x enters U(x) is decisive. In the dominant angle-encoding scheme, each feature x_j is written into a rotation angle, U(x) = product over layers of [ W_ent * (tensor_j R_{P_j}(c * x_j)) ], where R_P(a) = exp(-i a P / 2) is a Pauli rotation, W_ent is a fixed entangling layer, and c is a global scaling - the bandwidth. The choice of encoding controls the model's expressive power: repeating ("re-uploading") the data and stacking encodings enriches the function class, and one can show that the resulting model is a truncated Fourier series in x whose accessible frequencies are set by the encoding generators and their scaling [6, 7, 8]. The bandwidth c is therefore not a cosmetic knob: it sets the frequency content (and hence the effective length-scale) of the quantum feature map.

The community discovered, relatively recently, that bandwidth is the single most consequential hyperparameter for whether quantum kernels generalize at all. Without bandwidth control, quantum fidelity kernels suffer exponential concentration: for distinct inputs, the kernel value concentrates around a fixed constant with variance decaying exponentially in the number of qubits (or in an effective dimension), so the Gram matrix tends to the identity, every test point looks equidistant from every training point, generalization collapses, and an exponential number of measurement shots is needed merely to resolve off-diagonal entries [14, 23, 24]. Shaydulin and Wild showed empirically and theoretically that introducing a global bandwidth c and shrinking it restores generalization across standard feature maps [12]; Canatar and co-workers gave a spectral account showing that a single optimal bandwidth c* trades off two failure modes - too small a c drives K toward an all-ones (over-smoothing, under-resolving) matrix, while too large a c drives K toward the identity (concentration, no generalization) - and that tuning this one scalar is what enables learning [13]. Kuebler and colleagues analyzed the inductive bias that bandwidth imposes on the kernel spectrum [11], and systematic hyperparameter studies confirm bandwidth as the dominant control variable [20], with benchmarking suites operationalizing it as a tuned scalar [21]. The standard, current best practice is therefore: standardize the features, then select one global bandwidth c by cross-validation on pooled accuracy, and report that single number [12, 13, 20].

### 1.3 Trainable embeddings and the classical analog

A parallel line makes the embedding itself trainable: quantum embedding kernels and quantum metric learning optimize the parameters of the encoding circuit (for example by maximizing kernel-target alignment) so that same-class states cluster and different-class states separate [5, 15]. This adds expressive freedom but replaces one interpretable scalar with many free circuit parameters, inheriting trainability pathologies analogous to barren plateaus [23, 25] and providing no guarantee of reducing to a known, safe baseline. On the classical side, the idea that different input features deserve different length-scales is decades old: automatic relevance determination (ARD) gives each feature its own kernel length-scale in Gaussian-process and SVM models, learned from data, so that irrelevant features are effectively switched off [26, 27]. ARD is exactly a per-feature generalization of a single global length-scale - but it lives entirely in the classical kernel and says nothing about the quantum encoding layer or the quantum concentration phenomenon.

### 1.4 The sober counter-narrative

It is essential to be honest about quantum advantage. Strong recent results show that the very act of tuning a quantum kernel to generalize can erase any quantum advantage on classical data: Slattery and co-workers give extensive numerical evidence that the general-purpose hyperparameter tuning used to fix concentration drives the quantum kernel toward a classically well-approximable kernel, removing the possibility of advantage [18]; the universal-expressiveness results show variational classifiers and quantum kernels can represent the same function classes, so expressivity alone confers no separation [17]; and the power-of-data analysis explains when classical models with data can match quantum ones [9]. The contribution proposed here is deliberately scoped to live inside this counter-narrative: it improves quantum kernels relative to quantum baselines by fixing a self-inflicted representational inefficiency in how bandwidth is allocated, and it is simultaneously a diagnostic; it does not claim advantage over classical learners.

### 1.5 The gap this proposal targets

Across all of Section 1.2, the bandwidth is a single global scalar c multiplying every feature identically. Standardization removes raw per-feature scale, but it cannot remove per-feature differences in label-relevance, redundancy, or contribution to concentration. The pressing, unexamined question is whether the field's hard-won bandwidth - the one knob that makes quantum kernels work - is being left in a representationally crippled, one-number form, and whether promoting it to a per-feature quantity (without falling into the free-parameter trap of trainable embeddings) would recover accuracy that pooled tuning silently discards. That is the subject of this proposal.

---

## 2. Key Findings and Analysis

This section states the gap (ISOBAND), the method (APERTURE), the exact-collapse identity, and the four-part novelty wedge that the 5q in Section 3 builds on. The analysis is presented first as findings so that Section 3 can reference fixed notation.

### 2.1 Finding 1 - ISOBAND: one global bandwidth is representationally limited relative to the per-feature optimum

Define the gap ISOBAND (Isotropic-Bandwidth limit). In angle encoding the bandwidth enters as c * x_j with one shared c for all features j. After the standard unit-variance standardization, a concrete proxy for the encoded-state spread that drives concentration is the aggregate angular energy sum_j c_j^2 (equal to d * c^2 in the global-bandwidth case), because each feature's rotation contributes on the order of c_j^2 to the spread. Locally, to second order in small angle differences, the fidelity kernel obeys 1 - K(x, x') approximately equal to (1/2) * Delta^T M(c) Delta with Delta = x - x' and a positive-semidefinite matrix M(c) fixed by the encoding; in the additive, low-redundancy regime M(c) is approximately diagonal and this reduces to (1/2) * sum_j w_j * c_j^2 * (x_j - x'_j)^2, a quadratic form in which each feature's scaling c_j enters its own weight (w_j absorbs the generator and entangler factors), while in strongly entangled or correlated settings the off-diagonal cross terms of M(c) need not be negligible. Concentration is this aggregate quadratic form growing until distinct inputs become indistinguishable; generalization requires it to stay bounded. With one global c the only way to bound the aggregate is to bound c, but that same c sets the angular resolution c * (x_j - x'_j) of every feature, so it must be small enough for the many weakly-relevant or nuisance features that dominate the sum, and is then too small to resolve the few genuinely discriminative features, pushing their angular differences below the shot-noise floor. A single scalar therefore cannot simultaneously (a) keep the aggregate quadratic form out of concentration and (b) give each discriminative feature enough angular resolution.

In the regime where feature contributions are approximately additive and not strongly redundant, the per-feature optimal bandwidth c_j* is then non-constant whenever features differ in relevance, and one scalar cannot represent a non-constant vector; this is a representational limitation, not a tuning deficiency, that no value of the scalar c repairs. We state this as established in the additive, low-redundancy regime above, and as a hypothesis - to be tested empirically (Section 3, Question 5) - in the fully entangled, correlated, finite-shot regime, where strong feature redundancy, insensitive generators, or very smooth targets could still leave a global scalar near-optimal.

The harm has the signature the analysis predicts. It is SILENT under the standard pooled metric: the field selects the single c that maximizes pooled cross-validation accuracy and reports that number, which is by construction the best compromise; the misallocation only damages the minority of examples whose class signal lives in the under-resolved relevant features, while there is nothing to be gained on the over-budgeted nuisance features, so the two effects net out to a flat, healthy-looking pooled optimum. It SCALES with feature-relevance heterogeneity (anisotropy): the more the relevance/variance spectrum departs from uniform - as in essentially all real tabular and PCA-reduced image data, where a few features carry most of the discriminative signal - the larger the compromise loss. It VANISHES under isotropic features: when all features are equally relevant with equal variance, c_j* = c* for all j and the global bandwidth is optimal. The vanishing case is exactly the regime where the gap is undetectable and where, accordingly, the field has not noticed it.

### 2.2 Finding 2 - APERTURE: promote the scalar to a structured, partially-pooled, covariate-conditional map

Define the method APERTURE. Standardize features (zero mean, unit variance) - the established best practice that makes the global-bandwidth baseline strongest [12]. Then replace the global scalar with a per-feature bandwidth

    c_j = c_global * exp( g_theta(v_j) ),  clamped to [c_min, c_max],

where:

- v_j is an OBSERVABLE, leakage-free per-feature covariate measuring relevance - by default a cross-fitted absolute rank correlation |Spearman(x_j, y)| fused with a normalized mutual information estimate I(x_j; y), rank-normalized to a common range. It is computed on training folds only (cross-fitting), never uses test labels, and costs nothing on the quantum device.
- g_theta is a STRUCTURED MONOTONE non-decreasing map anchored at a reference relevance v0 (default: the median relevance) so that g_theta(v0) = 0, taking NEGATIVE values for below-anchor (low-relevance) features and POSITIVE values for above-anchor (high-relevance) features. It is parameterized as g_theta(v) = sum_k theta_k * ( phi_k(v) - phi_k(v0) ) with monotone non-decreasing basis functions phi_k (for example monotone splines or shifted softplus ramps) and non-negative coefficients theta_k. Thus the map is monotone, carries no surviving constant offset, and - crucially - theta = 0 makes g_theta(v) = 0 for every v because every learnable coefficient vanishes. The anchoring lets nuisance features fall below c_global and discriminative features rise above it, rather than the map only ever increasing c_j.
- Partial pooling: the deployable DEFAULT is a single global monotone map (one shared theta and anchor v0 for all features), and per-feature covariates v_j are shrunk toward the global mean relevance in proportion to their estimation reliability, so noisy or data-poor features default to c_global. An OPTIONAL hierarchical empirical-Bayes extension fits bin-level maps across K relevance bins that are themselves shrunk toward the global map; it is a strictly richer model class used only when validation supports it [28, 29].

The monotone direction encodes a principled but explicitly TESTED inductive bias rather than a physical necessity: more relevant features are given a LARGER bandwidth (more angular resolution) and nuisance features a SMALLER one (squashed toward the identity). The mechanism is budget reallocation - because the aggregate angular energy sum_j c_j^2 controls concentration (Section 2.1), shrinking the many nuisance features lowers the aggregate and frees headroom to raise c_j on the few discriminative features without the aggregate entering concentration, a trade no single global c can make. Whether higher relevance truly warrants a larger bandwidth depends on target smoothness, feature noise, redundancy, and the circuit generators, so the proposal tests the direction directly (reversed-direction and non-monotone ablations, the monotone-dose falsifier, and the covariate-scramble placebo in Question 5) rather than assuming it. To keep the procedure leakage-free, the entire pipeline - the covariate estimates v_j, the anchor v0, the bins, the pooling, the clamp bounds, theta, and c_global - is fit strictly inside each training fold of nested cross-validation and frozen before any validation or test kernel entry is computed; no test point or test label ever enters v_j or the c_j. The c_j are then fixed precomputed constants, so U(x) remains unitary and K remains a valid positive-semidefinite fidelity kernel, and APERTURE touches only the classical prefactors of the encoding rotations, adding zero quantum cost (identical gate count, identical circuit topology, identical shot budget).

### 2.3 Finding 3 - the exact-collapse identity

APERTURE contains the global-bandwidth quantum kernel as an exact special case. At theta = 0 the anchored, no-surviving-offset construction gives g_theta(v) = 0 for ALL v (every learnable coefficient vanishes and the anchored basis carries no fixed offset), hence c_j = c_global for every feature (and the clamp is inactive whenever c_global lies in [c_min, c_max]). Then U_APERTURE(x) = U_base(x) for every x, so the encoded states, the fidelity kernel, the Gram matrix, the SVM dual solution, and the predictions are all identical, term for term. The collapse is enforced as a continuous-integration gate:

    E_collapse = max over (x, x') in train+validation of | K_APERTURE(x, x') - K_base(x, x') |  <=  1e-6,

verifiable by construction (the c_j are deterministic functions of theta; theta = 0 yields exact equality up to floating point). Setting additionally c_global = c* recovers the Shaydulin-Wild / Canatar state-of-the-art global-bandwidth kernel exactly [12, 13]. APERTURE is therefore a strict, shadow-deployable generalization with a do-no-harm default: at worst it equals the current best practice; it can only differ from it where the learned map is non-trivial.

### 2.4 Finding 4 - the four-part novelty wedge C1-C4

APERTURE is defined by four simultaneous properties:

- C1 (encoding-layer only, zero quantum cost): operates purely on the data-encoding angle-scaling. No change to ansatz topology, entangling structure, qubit count, layer count, the overlap/kernel-estimation circuit, the shot budget, or the downstream classical SVM. The c_j are d classical constants from a one-time O(n*d) training-statistics pre-pass.
- C2 (observable covariate): conditions the bandwidth on an observable, leakage-free per-feature relevance covariate v_j, cross-fitted from training data.
- C3 (structured, partially-pooled map): a structured monotone map - by default a single global map, optionally hierarchically partially-pooled across relevance bins - which is neither a single global scalar (that is ISOBAND) nor unpooled free per-feature parameters (that is trainable-embedding/ARD).
- C4 (exact collapse): provable exact reduction to the global-bandwidth quantum kernel at theta = 0, gated by E_collapse <= 1e-6.

The central novelty claim, established in Section 3 Question 4, is that No prior line covers more than two of C1-C4. The collapse target (global bandwidth) has C1 but lacks C2 and C3; trainable embedding kernels have C1 but lack C2, C3, and C4; classical ARD has a per-feature notion but lacks C1, C3, and C4. APERTURE is the first construction to hold all four.

### 2.5 Why this matters as analysis

The findings reframe bandwidth - currently treated as a scalar hyperparameter to be searched - as a representational object: a degenerate constant special case of a monotone, partially-pooled map of an observable per-feature covariate. The visible symptom of the degeneracy (accuracy lost on the anisotropic example tail, and an over-concentrated Gram matrix relative to what a per-feature allocation could achieve at matched aggregate spread) is hidden by pooled cross-validation accuracy, which is exactly why the gap has persisted through the very papers that established bandwidth's importance.

---

## 3. Novel Research Idea (5q)

Here is a high-level summarized insight of a research field Machine Learning. The current best practice for quantum kernel methods restores generalization by tuning one global bandwidth scalar that scales every input feature identically in the data-encoding circuit; this proposal shows that single scalar is a degenerate special case of a structured, partially-pooled, observable-covariate-conditional per-feature bandwidth, and that promoting it - while provably collapsing back to the global-bandwidth kernel at its default - recovers accuracy that pooled tuning silently discards on feature-heterogeneous data.

Here are the five core questions:

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

Can the single global bandwidth that quantum kernel methods use to scale all input features identically in the data-encoding circuit be replaced by a per-feature bandwidth - a structured, monotone, partially-pooled map of an observable per-feature relevance covariate - that improves classification on feature-heterogeneous (anisotropic) data while provably collapsing to the global-bandwidth quantum kernel at its default setting?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

Bandwidth is the hinge on which modern quantum kernel methods turn. The results that revived quantum kernels after the discovery of exponential concentration did so by introducing exactly one scalar and tuning it [12, 13], and subsequent hyperparameter studies confirm it is the dominant control variable [20]. Showing that this load-bearing scalar is a degenerate special case of a per-feature map - and that the promotion is essentially free (encoding-layer only, zero added quantum cost, exact collapse to the current best practice) - changes how the community should think about the single most important design choice in the field. The implications are broad and concrete:

- It upgrades the field's central hyperparameter into a representational object. Instead of searching for one best c, practitioners would fit a monotone, partially-pooled allocation of bandwidth across features, with the old scalar recovered as the theta = 0 default. Because the default is exactly today's state of the art, the method is safely adoptable: any group already tuning a global bandwidth can switch on APERTURE with an exact do-no-harm default at theta = 0 (the baseline is recovered identically), so adoption risks nothing relative to current practice while offering measured upside on anisotropic data; an active (non-default) configuration is selected only when validation supports it and can in principle be outperformed, in which case cross-validation simply returns the theta = 0 default. This lowers the barrier to adoption to nearly zero and makes the contribution immediately useful rather than aspirational.

- It connects two previously disjoint literatures - the quantum bandwidth/concentration line [9, 11, 12, 13, 14, 24] and the classical automatic-relevance-determination line [26, 27] - through a single construction, and it does so without importing ARD's free-parameter cost or its blindness to the quantum encoding layer. This cross-pollination is the kind that reshapes how a subfield frames its problems: it suggests that other global QML hyperparameters (re-uploading depth, generator choice, entangler strength) may likewise be degenerate scalars awaiting covariate-conditional, exact-collapse generalizations.

- It is a diagnostic as much as a method. The anisotropy-stratified evaluation, Gram-concentration diagnostics, and covariate-scramble / isotropic-null / monotone-dose falsifiers that the proposal introduces are reusable instruments for detecting representational blind spots that pooled accuracy hides. Future benchmarking efforts [21] could adopt them to report not just a tuned accuracy number but how much of a model's headroom a single global bandwidth leaves on the table.

- It sharpens, rather than evades, the advantage debate. Because the strongest evidence is that global bandwidth tuning can push quantum kernels toward classically simulable ones [18], a principled account of how to allocate bandwidth per feature clarifies precisely where any residual quantum structure lives and where it is being averaged away. Even a negative outcome (APERTURE helps only marginally on real data) is informative: it would localize the limits of quantum kernels to the encoding's representational budget rather than to tuning effort. Either way the paper advances knowledge and equips practitioners with a drop-in, exactly-reversible improvement to the field's most consequential knob, with direct practical payoff on the heterogeneous tabular and PCA-reduced image data that dominate applied QML [1, 19, 20, 21].

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

The difficulty is that the obvious fixes either do not change the representation or destroy the properties that make the fix safe and detectable. Six obstacles structure the problem; the last is binding.

- O1 - the per-feature optimum is entangled, not separable. Feature relevance is confounded with feature variance, with inter-feature redundancy/correlation, and - critically - with the nonlinear coupling induced by the entangling gates, so features interact and the optimal bandwidth vector c_j* is not a product of independent per-feature solutions. A naive per-feature line search (optimize each c_j alone) is therefore wrong, because changing one feature's bandwidth shifts the aggregate spread and hence the concentration budget available to all others. The method must use a deliberately constrained, structured map plus pooling to approximate the coupled optimum rather than chase d independent optima.

- O2 - data poverty per feature. There are only d features, often spread thinly across the relevance spectrum, and per-feature relevance estimates v_j are noisy. Unconstrained per-feature bandwidths (one free parameter each) overfit the kernel to the training split, especially under finite-shot noise. This is why partial pooling is mandatory rather than optional: noisy v_j and data-poor relevance bins must shrink toward the global default [28, 29].

- O3 - observability and leakage-freeness. The covariate must be computable from training data alone, must not peek at test labels (or the evaluation is circular), and must be cheap - it cannot require running the quantum device or solving an inner SVM per feature. Cross-fitting the relevance estimate and freezing the c_j as constants before kernel construction is necessary to keep the procedure honest and the kernel valid.

- O4 - heterogeneity is multi-axis. "Relevance" is not one thing: a feature can be high-variance but label-irrelevant (a nuisance to squash), low-variance but decisive, or redundant with another. A scalar covariate is itself a compromise, so the covariate (and the binning) must combine scale, label-relevance, and redundancy, while remaining low-dimensional enough to pool.

- O5 - simultaneous lift, exact collapse, and kernel validity. The method must improve the anisotropic tail AND provably reduce to the global-bandwidth kernel at the default AND keep the kernel positive-semidefinite. These pull against one another: expressive per-feature freedom threatens both collapse and PSD-ness. The no-constant-term monotone map (for exact collapse) and the fixed-constant c_j (for PSD validity) are specific design choices forced by this triple constraint; a generic trainable embedding satisfies none of the three.

- O6 (BINDING) - metric blindness. Even a correct per-feature bandwidth shows no benefit under the field's standard yardstick. Pooled cross-validation accuracy at the single best global c is, by construction, a compromise optimum; the ISOBAND harm concentrates on a minority example tail and cancels in the mean, so the pooled number looks healthy whether or not the misallocation is present. Without anisotropy-stratified evaluation, Gram-matrix concentration diagnostics, and the covariate-scramble / isotropic-null / monotone-dose falsifiers introduced here, the gap is literally undetectable - which is why naive approaches not only fail to fix it but cannot even see it. This binding obstacle explains the otherwise puzzling fact that the papers establishing bandwidth's importance [11, 12, 13, 20] did not notice its per-feature degeneracy: their instrument could not register it.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

The gap sits in a blind spot created by the history and the measurement practice of the subfield, and every neighboring line misses at least two of the wedge properties C1-C4 (C1 encoding-layer-only/zero-quantum-cost; C2 observable per-feature covariate; C3 structured partially-pooled map; C4 exact collapse to the global-bandwidth kernel).

- NM1 - global-bandwidth quantum kernels (Shaydulin-Wild [12], Canatar [13], the inductive-bias analysis [11], hyperparameter studies [20]). This is the collapse target. It has C1 (it scales the encoding) but uses a single scalar, missing C2 and C3. (Misses C2, C3.)

- NM2 (sharpest) - trainable / variational quantum embedding kernels and quantum metric learning (Lloyd et al. quantum embeddings [5], Hubregtsen et al. [15]). These make the embedding adaptive, but through FREE variational circuit parameters trained by kernel-target alignment. They arguably satisfy C1, but miss C2 (parameters are free, not a structured map of an observable covariate), C3 (unpooled free parameters, not a partially-pooled monotone map), and C4 (they carry no enforced exact-collapse identity to a known global-bandwidth kernel; a particular trained embedding might happen to include a global-bandwidth setting, but nothing guarantees or gates a reduction to it, and the trained circuit inherits trainability pathologies [23, 25]). (Misses C2, C3, C4.)

- NM3 - classical automatic relevance determination (ARD) kernels and ARD Gaussian processes (Rasmussen-Williams [26], Neal [27]). ARD is the conceptual ancestor: per-feature length-scales learned from data. But it is purely classical - it lives in the classical kernel, addresses neither the quantum encoding layer nor quantum concentration, and has no notion of collapsing to a quantum global-bandwidth kernel; although hierarchical or pooled ARD variants exist (which soften the C3 gap), ARD unambiguously misses C1 (it never touches a quantum encoding layer or addresses quantum concentration) and C4 (no exact collapse to a quantum global-bandwidth kernel). (Misses at least C1 and C4.)

- NM4 - data re-uploading and expressive-encoding design (Perez-Salinas [6], Schuld-Sweke-Meyer Fourier analysis [8], quantum circuit learning [7]). These enrich the encoding's global frequency spectrum (more re-uploads, different generators), changing the function class globally rather than allocating a per-feature bandwidth conditioned on an observable covariate; there is no exact collapse to a scalar-bandwidth kernel. (Misses C2, C3, C4.)

- NM5 - standard global-bandwidth quantum-kernel hyperparameter search and benchmarking (Egginger et al. [20], Bowles et al. [21]). These SELECT a single global bandwidth (and other global hyperparameters) by grid or Bayesian search on pooled accuracy, not a per-feature covariate-conditional bandwidth. The output is a single global value, not a covariate-conditional map, with no structured pooling and no collapse framing. (Misses C2, C3, C4.)

- NM6 - covariant / group-structured quantum kernels (Glick et al. [16]). These tailor the feature map to a KNOWN data group symmetry - a powerful but global, symmetry-keyed construction that requires the symmetry to be known and given, is not a per-feature observable-statistic bandwidth map, and does not collapse to a scalar-bandwidth kernel. (Misses C2, C3, C4.)

Thus No prior line covers more than two of C1-C4. The barrier has three reinforcing parts. First, history: bandwidth was only recognized as the cure for concentration in 2021-2023 [12, 13], and was immediately cast as a single global knob to be tuned, not as a representational choice. Second, a fork in the road: the one community that did make the encoding adaptive (trainable embeddings [5, 15]) went all the way to free variational parameters, abandoning both the observable-covariate structure and the exact-collapse safety, while the ARD idea that would have supplied the per-feature structure stayed on the classical side of the fence [26, 27]. Third, and binding, measurement: pooled cross-validation accuracy hid the per-feature misallocation (Question 3, O6), so there was no empirical pressure to look for it. APERTURE differs by occupying exactly the unclaimed intersection - an encoding-layer-only (C1), observable-covariate-conditioned (C2), structured partially-pooled monotone map (C3) with provable exact collapse to the global-bandwidth kernel (C4) - and by bringing the anisotropy-stratified, falsifier-backed instrumentation needed to make the previously invisible gap measurable.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

Method. Standardize features. Compute the observable per-feature covariate v_j by default as a cross-fitted fusion of absolute rank correlation |Spearman(x_j, y)| and normalized mutual information I(x_j; y), rank-normalized to a common range (alternatives ablated: HSIC, Fisher score, variance-only). Set the per-feature bandwidth c_j = c_global * exp(g_theta(v_j)), clamped to [c_min, c_max] (default band 0.1*c_global to 10*c_global), where g_theta is the anchored monotone map of Section 2.2 (anchored at the median relevance v0 with g_theta(v0) = 0, monotone basis with non-negative coefficients, no surviving offset, so theta = 0 implies g_theta identically 0 and nuisance features fall below c_global while discriminative features rise above it). The deployable default is a single global map; an optional empirical-Bayes hierarchical variant adds bin-level maps across K = 5 relevance bins shrunk toward the global map, with reliability-weighted shrinkage of v_j, so noisy or data-poor features default to c_global [28, 29]. Fit the entire pipeline (v_j, the anchor v0, the bins, the pooling, the clamp bounds, theta, and c_global) strictly inside each inner training fold of nested cross-validation, on the same pooled validation objective the baseline uses (inner loop may use kernel-target alignment for speed), and freeze it before any validation or test kernel entry is computed; we report the fraction of features sitting at a clamp bound as a diagnostic (a large fraction signals an over-tight band). Build the fidelity kernel on a statevector simulator with finite-shot sampling (PennyLane / Qiskit) [22], over a qubit sweep n = 4..16 to span concentration onset; an optional small real-hardware run is reported with explicit NISQ-noise caveats.

Datasets. (a) Standard quantum-kernel benchmarks with heterogeneous feature relevance: UCI / OpenML tabular sets used in the hyperparameter and benchmarking studies [20, 21], MNIST and Fashion-MNIST reduced by PCA to n features [1, 19], and the structured "ad hoc" feature-map dataset where the quantum kernel is designed to be advantageous (positive control) [1]. (b) Semi-synthetic controlled-anisotropy data: k informative features plus (d - k) nuisance features with a tunable relevance-spread parameter rho, giving ground-truth per-feature relevance, a dose-response axis in rho, and an isotropic null at rho = 0.

Baselines. B1 classical SVM-RBF and classical ARD-GP (per-feature length-scale) for context only - not an advantage claim [26, 27]; B2 quantum kernel with no bandwidth (raw encoding), exhibiting concentration [14]; B3 standardized quantum kernel with the best global bandwidth (Shaydulin-Wild / Canatar), the PRIMARY collapse target and identity guard [12, 13]; B4 per-feature inverse-variance standardization only (fixed 1/sqrt(var), no label, no learned map, no pooling); B5 a RETUNED best-global bandwidth fit on the isotropic-null set, used for the isotropic-null falsifier and kept distinct from the theta = 0 identity guard; B6 unpooled free per-feature bandwidths / a trainable embedding kernel [15] (overfit/expensive control, no exact collapse); B7 covariate-scrambled APERTURE (v_j permuted across features), a placebo; B8 a per-feature oracle bandwidth from Bayesian optimization (impractical upper bound); B9 APERTURE.

Metrics. PRIMARY: classification accuracy and ROC-AUC on the high-anisotropy datasets and on the under-resolved-feature example tail, plus, on semi-synthetic data, recovery of the known relevance ordering and the dose-response slope in rho. SECONDARY: pooled no-regression versus B3 within a pre-registered margin (bootstrap 95% confidence interval or 1% relative, whichever is larger); train-test generalization gap; a Gram-matrix concentration diagnostic defined precisely as the off-diagonal mean mu_off and variance Var_off of the kernel over distinct pairs together with the Gram matrix effective rank / spectral flatness (less concentration meaning larger Var_off and larger effective rank), where the comparison to B3 is made at matched aggregate spread - B3's global bandwidth is set so that its mean off-diagonal Hilbert-Schmidt distance (equivalently its mean value of 1 - K over distinct pairs) equals APERTURE's, isolating the effect of ALLOCATION from overall scale; shot-efficiency (resolvability of kernel entries at fixed shots); kernel-target alignment; and the exact-collapse gate E_collapse <= 1e-6. Ablations A1-A8 vary the covariate (corr vs MI vs HSIC vs Fisher vs variance-only), the monotone-basis family, K, the pooling strength, the clamp band, standardize-first on/off, the qubit count (concentration onset vs anisotropy gain), and the map DIRECTION (the default increasing map versus reversed and non-monotone maps, directly testing the inductive bias of Section 2.2). Falsifiers: T1 isotropic-null - on whitened, equal-relevance features APERTURE must NOT beat the RETUNED best-global B5 (this is distinct from, and stronger than, the theta = 0 identity guard, because a constant covariate yields a shifted global bandwidth, not the base kernel, unless theta = 0); T2 monotone-dose - the gain must grow monotonically with the anisotropy parameter rho; T3 covariate-scramble - permuting v_j (B7) must destroy the gain.

Expected outcomes (hypotheses; all numeric ranges are conjectured with no preliminary results). H1: on high-anisotropy real and semi-synthetic data, APERTURE (B9) improves anisotropy-tail accuracy / AUC over the global-bandwidth collapse target B3 by a margin that grows with anisotropy, with the largest gains at intermediate qubit counts where concentration is biting but not yet total. H2: pooled accuracy does not regress versus B3 beyond the pre-registered margin on any dataset (do-no-harm). H3: APERTURE yields a measurably less concentrated Gram matrix (higher off-diagonal variance) than B3 at matched aggregate spread, with better shot-efficiency. H4: covariate-scramble (B7) erases at least the large majority (target >= 80%) of APERTURE's gain, and the unpooled control B6 overfits (good training alignment, degraded test accuracy), confirming that structure and pooling, not mere per-feature freedom, drive the effect. H5: the dose-response slope in rho is positive and the isotropic-null T1 shows no gain over the retuned global B5. H6: E_collapse <= 1e-6 holds at theta = 0 and APERTURE reproduces B3 exactly there - verifiable by construction. The headline deliverable is a drop-in, exactly-reversible upgrade to the field's most important hyperparameter, plus a reusable anisotropy-stratified, falsifier-backed diagnostic for representational blind spots in quantum kernels - with an explicit, honest scoping that the contribution is relative to quantum baselines and is not a claim of advantage over classical learners [9, 17, 18].

Responsible AI and Data-Governance Considerations. F1 - covariate governance and sensitive-feature amplification: because APERTURE allocates more bandwidth to higher-relevance features, a feature that is or proxies a sensitive attribute could have its influence amplified; the protocol audits v_j against protected features, supports pinning or penalizing protected-feature bandwidth, and computes v_j only from aggregate training statistics (data minimization). F2 - fairness in evaluation: anisotropy-stratified results are also disaggregated by class so that minority-class discriminative features are not silently squashed; per-class metrics are reported alongside pooled ones. F3 - do-no-harm default: theta = 0 is an exact disable switch recovering the state-of-the-art global-bandwidth kernel; APERTURE is shadow-deployable, and any active (non-default) configuration is validation-gated. F4 - scientific integrity: all numeric ranges above are marked conjectured with no preliminary results; the E_collapse unit test, the semi-synthetic anisotropy generators, and simulation seeds are released; and the NISQ caveat is stated plainly - hardware noise interacts with concentration, so simulator gains may not transfer, and hardware claims are spot-checked [24]. F5 - no-advantage honesty and auditability: the paper states explicitly that APERTURE improves quantum kernels relative to quantum baselines by closing a self-inflicted representational gap and does not claim advantage over classical machine learning - consistent with evidence that well-tuned quantum kernels can become classically approximable [18], that variational classifiers and quantum kernels are universally expressive [17], and that data can empower classical models [9]; per-feature bandwidths c_j, covariates v_j, the map parameters theta, and the clamp band are logged for auditability.
